entry.title: Breaking Down Silos to Unified Customer Data — Powered by the Advanced Success Plan for SAP CX
entry.content:
More Tools, More Fragmentation

Each day, businesses invest in new software tools: marketing platforms, commerce engines, service systems, and sales technology. They are told that assembling the right combination will unlock their digital transformation journey and finally deliver that personalized and seamless experience they wish to provide to their customers.

But what if more or better software is not necessarily the answer?

Each solution solves a real problem for a specific team. But collectively, they create a consequence no one planned for: every new tool builds its own data world, without a common language or context across them. In a 2026 study by Oxford Economics, only 25% of respondents described their CX technology environment as fully harmonized and integrated. 29% remain highly fragmented. Organizations with siloed CX tech are more likely to face an inability to connect customer needs to actionable data insights. 58% reported this challenge, compared to 47% among those with harmonized environments.

More than what tools businesses choose to add to their CX landscape, how they connect and interact with each other becomes even more important. A unified data strategy sounds straightforward in principle. In practice, most businesses find that the obstacle is not ambition but rather execution. Every integration decision made without a clear data architecture becomes a future campaign mired in manual reconciliation, a customer journey that breaks at the handoff, or a personalization promise the disparate data sources cannot support. Implementation without a validated strategy creates new fragmentation inside the solution meant to eliminate the old. And without a structured way to pressure-test decisions before any commitment is made, even well-resourced organizations find themselves repeating the same cycle: invest, integrate, fragment, repeat.

A Better Way to Build

The real barrier is not budget or technology.

A Forrester study of 1,000+ senior executives found that data quality and integration issues are cited as the top cause (39%) of delayed or derailed transformation projects, more than any other factor such as budget, technology maturity, and talent. The same study found that 56% of respondents struggle with poor data quality; 55% face persistent data silos. This is not for lack of investment in technology, but because the connections between systems were not designed or maintained effectively.

What bridges that gap is not another platform — it is the expertise to think through data connectivity decisions before they are made, and the ongoing guidance to ensure those decisions compound into measurable gains over time. The Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience solutions provides that guidance along every step of the journey.

Define what success actually looks like

The most common reason data unification projects fall short of expectations is not technical failure – it is a failure to define and measure what success looks like for the entire business before the work begins. The Value Management session from the Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience establishes that definition at the outset: what does a fully unified data strategy actually enable? It means running the next marketing campaign without manual data reconciliation, and presenting an AI readiness roadmap without caveats. Stakeholders will know, at every checkpoint, whether the investment is moving the business forward, not just moving the project forward.

Design the strategy before building the integrations

A robust data strategy starts with good design. Product Guidance from the Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience covers available out-of-the-box integrations, common usage scenarios, and pitfalls and how to avoid them — all delivered in a live remote session by an SAP expert who can answer questions in real time. The data strategy can be conceptualized and pressure-tested before any budget or technical commitments are made.

Validate every critical decision with expert guidance

With the Technical Assistance and Functional Assistance from the Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience, businesses have continuous access to expert guidance at every critical decision point. Beyond resolving immediate questions, the ongoing access also shares insight on how SAP thinks through problems, strengthening in-house expertise with every interaction. The result is an organization that makes better decisions not just now but for the future.

Measure whether the strategy is delivering

Adoption and Innovation Checkpoints conducted on a quarterly or semi-annual basis bring the measurement back to where it started: the business outcomes defined at the outset. Do campaigns run without manual reconciliation? Is the AI use case performing against its stated goals? A clear throughline from the Value Management success KPIs to the Adoption and Innovation Checkpoints proves the benefits of the investment.

Where cycles and silos break

The cycle of invest, integrate, fragment, repeat is not inevitable. It is the predictable result of making technical decisions without an anchoring business imperative, and integration decisions without strategic expert guidance. Organizations that break the cycle do not necessarily have better technology than their competitors — they have better judgment about how to use it. The Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience exists for exactly that reason: to put proactive and prescriptive guidance at every decision point where that judgment matters most. When data strategy is designed before it is built, validated before it is committed, and measured against real business outcomes, the technology investment already made starts working harder. The stack was never the problem. When the thinking behind the technology finally matches its ambition, the personalized, seamless experience becomes a reality.

Where is your organization in the invest, integrate, fragment, repeat cycle? What would it require to break the cycle organizationally, not just technically?

entry.title: Linked POBs
entry.content:
A linked performance obligation (POB) is an implicitly promised item. It is used when you want an item in RA contract which doesn’t have a corresponding item in the sales document. This blog will act as a guide to the creation of a linked POB. It includes the exact configuration steps and price allocation among POBs with a detailed example. 

 A linked POB is created in reference to a leading POB. There are two ways to create a Linked POB:

BRF+ configuration

In Decision Table Process add. POB

Example:

Created an order with 2 line items in SD

In RAIMON, we see two POBs

In contract, we see there’s a third POB created wrt the first POB

POB structure UI

Adding a linked POB using NWBC

Price Allocation

Scenario 1: Where SSP is not defined for the linked POB

Scenario 2: Where SSP is defined for the Linked POB

To note:

In optimized Contract Management, the leading POB cannot be time-based.​

Examples:

  1. A software license along with installation service. Installation is an implicitly promised item along with a software, which if not included in a contract explicitly is needed as a linked POB

Software license- Leading POB
Installation service- Linked POB

2. A water filter and Annual maintenance service with it which isn’t a separate line in a contract is a linked POB with the water filter as the leading POB

Water filter- Leading POB
Annual Maintenance service- Linked POB

Conclusion

To conclude, Linked POB is a useful feature which can be setup easily with the right approach. I hope the configuration steps and allocation logic covered in the post help you with your implementation. 

Do let me know in the comments below if you have any questions about linked POBs!

P.S. This isn't an AI-generated content.

entry.title: Custom Fields with Field Status Group control in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
One of the most significant changes introduced by SAP S/4HANA is the unification of financial tables into a single source of truth: the Universal Journal, or table ACDOCA. With this new table, creating new fields is no longer an exclusive task for ABAP developers. Through SAP Fiori, functional consultants now have access to an application that allows us to create custom fields and assign them to Field Status Groups.

Depending on the intended business use of the field, it can be populated automatically using a BAdI (BADI_FINS_ACDOC_POSTING_EVENTS) or manually during journal entry posting.

In this blog, I will explain through a practical example how to create a custom field available for data entry during General Journal Entries posting. The allowed values for this field will be restricted by a drop-down list (Code List), and its field status requirement (Required/Optional/Hidden) will be controlled by the Field Status Group.

Creating the Custom Field

To create a custom field, we must use the SAP Fiori app Custom Fields (App ID: F1481😞

Upon opening the app, select the "Create" (plus icon) button located in the upper right corner:

A pop-up dialog will appear with the following fields:

Since the new field must be available for data entry when creating a G/L journal entry, you must select FINS_CODING_BLOCK in the Business Context field:

Enter the label for the new field and select the field type from the drop-down list. For this exercise, this custom field should only be populated during posting using specific values, so we select the Code List option. Selecting this option enables new configuration fields within the pop-up to define the drop-down values:

  • Length: Defines the character length of the drop-down values during journal entry creation, which directly determines the technical length of the custom field.
  • List Values: Use this section to manage the dropdown values and their corresponding descriptions.

After filling in all required fields, select the Create button at the bottom right of the pop-up window. To publish the field, navigate to the details page:

Important: Since this field will be controlled via the Field Status Group (Transaction OBC4), it is not necessary to enable user interfaces.

To finalize the field creation process, proceed to publish it by clicking the Publish button at the bottom right of the screen. Once published, the field is successfully created and will exist within table ACDOCA:

Including the Field in the Coding Block

This activity must be performed via SAP GUI, either by navigating through SPRO to the Customizing activity Edit Coding Block or by directly executing transaction code OXK3:

First and foremost, this activity allows the inclusion of custom fields into FI General Ledger accounts, enabling them within the Field Status Groups. It also allows fields to be integrated into MM Stock Management, MM Purchasing, and CO Line Items.

For our example, this process will enable the new custom field on an FI subscreen, making it visible and available during journal entry posting.

Upon accessing this customizing activity, the transaction will display two options. We must select Customer–Defined Account Assignments, and within it, Customer Include Structure. This is where the CI_COBL structure is located, which we will use to link our newly created custom field:

To add the field, double-click on the CI_COBL structure and populate only the Component and Component Type fields using the exact field identifier generated in the Custom Fields SAP Fiori app:

Proceed to save the changes. Then, double-click on the structure once again to activate the new field:

Once the activation process is executed, an activation log will be generated where you can review any occurring errors. Typically, two errors are displayed: one related to table COEP and another related to table MSEG:

To resolve the error associated with table COEP, follow the instructions provided in KBA 2384731 - Correction program: COEP and replacement object V_COEP have different number of columns. This SAP Note will instruct you to execute the report FCO_CDS_VIEW_GENERATE. Once this program has run successfully, the new custom field will be visible in the V_COEP view:

To resolve the error associated with table MSEG, follow the instructions provided in KBA 2242679 - Redirect inconsistency - Proxy Substitution. The assistance of an ABAP developer will be required to fix this error. Since this is a walkthrough example, we can ignore the error in table MSEG and proceed; however, it is critical to always resolve all occurring errors to prevent future data inconsistencies in the production environment.

Creating the Coding Block Subscreen

Once the errors have been resolved and the CI_COBL structure is fully activated, the account assignment subscreen must be created. This configuration activity can be accessed either via transaction code OXK3 or by directly executing transaction OXK1.

Upon executing the transaction, select the "Create" (New) option. The system will prompt you to create a new subscreen number within the allowed custom range of 9000 – 9999:

The new field will be available, and you will only need to assign a position number to it and generate the screen:

The new subscreen will be created under the Customer Subscreens section:

If the new custom field is missing from the subscreen you are creating, follow the recommendations provided in KBA 3609989 - Added Z Coding Block Field Not Available on OXK1 Screen.

Updating the Field Status Group

This is the final activity required to complete the end-to-end setup. The first task that must be performed—not due to a strict process sequence, but because it is commonly overlooked—is to change the field status of the new custom field within the Posting Keys that will be used for the corresponding accounts. This is maintained via transaction OB41 (Posting Keys). For this exercise, the changes were applied only to posting keys 40 (Debit) and 50 (Credit).

The second task is to update the Field Status Variant where the field status group assigned to the target G/L accounts is located. This configuration activity can be accessed via the SPRO reference IMG menu path or by directly executing transaction code OBC4:

Once inside the variant and the specific field status group, you must select the "Additional account assignments" field group:

If the technical name of the new field appears as "Subgroup text is missing", you must follow the resolution steps outlined in KBA 3592675 - OBC4: the short name is "Subgroup text is missing":

Once the steps suggested in KBA 3592675 are completed, the field name will be displayed correctly. You can then proceed to select the required field status (e.g., Optional or Required entry) and save the changes:

Testing with a Journal Entry Document

Once all system preparation and customizing activities are completed, we can post financial documents using the new custom field.

For our example, we will execute the SAP Fiori app Post General Journal Entries (App ID: F0718) and select a G/L account that has the modified field status group assigned within its Master Data. Within the line item details, select the "More" subscreen button, where you will see the new custom field available with its drop-down list:

Conclusion

Throughout this document, we've seen that this approach to managing custom fields is specific to general ledger postings. Using custom fields in other modules might require ABAP support. We've verified that we can manage the creation of these fields from start to finish and even enable them in field status groups. I hope this post is helpful for all functional consultants who enjoy exploring the limits of SAP.

entry.title: Field Validation in SAP SuccessFactors Performance Management Using Business Rules
entry.content:
During a recent SuccessFactors Performance Management implementation, a customer wanted to ensure that managers provide comments whenever an employee receives a low performance rating. This requirement was addressed using Business Rules to validate the performance form before submission.

Business Requirement:

In one of our SAP SuccessFactors Performance Management implementations, the customer wanted managers to rate only 3 competencies out of a list of 10+ competency items during the Manager Assessment step.

If a manager rated fewer than 3 competencies or more than 3 competencies, the system should display an error message and prevent the form from moving forward.

Solution:

To address this requirement, we leveraged the Get Count of Ratings function available in SAP SuccessFactors Business Rules.
The rule was configured to count the number of competency ratings provided by the manager within the Competency Section. The validation was performed on:

Form.Form Section.Competencies.Official Rating

Since the rating scale ranged from 1 to 5, any competency with a rating greater than 0 was considered as a rated competency.
The business rule counted the total number of rated competencies and validated whether the count was exactly 3.

Error Message:

"Please rate exactly 3 competencies before submitting the performance form."

A key challenge was determining when the validation should be triggered. If the rule was configured solely based on the rating count, the error message would appear throughout the entire performance review process, starting from the initial form stage.

To avoid this behavior, we used option to define the stage:
 Form.Route Map.Current Step 

This allowed us to restrict the validation to the specific Manager Assessment step, ensuring that the error message was displayed only when the manager was expected to complete the competency ratings.

Benefits:

This approach is particularly useful when performance forms contain a large number of competencies or evaluation items.

Key benefits include:

  • Ensures compliance with business requirements.
  • Improves consistency in manager evaluations.
  • Prevents overrating or underrating competency selections.

Conclusion:

Using the Get Count of Ratings function provides a simple and effective way to enforce rating limits within SAP SuccessFactors Performance Management. Business Rules provide an efficient way to enforce validation requirements in Performance Management without custom development. Properly designed rules help organizations improve compliance and maintain high-quality performance review data.

entry.title: Re: ASN quantity in Cumulative received Qty in the inboun...
entry.content:
Do not overwrite SAP’s cumulative delivered qty with customer’s cumulative received qty. Instead:

  • Keep SAP standard logic intact (PGI-driven).

  • Store customer’s cumulative received qty in a custom field/report.

  • Use reconciliation reports to bridge the difference.

This way, you avoid breaking SAP’s scheduling agreement logic while still honoring the customer’s reporting requirement.

entry.title: Banking Payments paid Invoices posting dates of Invoices Query
entry.content:
Hi All

Please guide me to join INV1 to get GL Account Number in this Query.

 

SELECT T0.DocNum, T0.JrnlMemo, T0.DocType, T0.Canceled, T0.DocDate, T0.CardCode, T0.DocCurr, T0.DocTotal, T0.DocTotalFC, T2.DocNum as 'Invoice Number', T2.DocDate as 'Invoice Date', T2.JrnlMemo, T2.DocTotal as 'Original Invoice Amount', T3.DocNum as 'Credit Note Number', T3.DocTotal as 'Original Credit Note Amount', T4.TransId as 'JE Transaction Number', T3.JrnlMemo,  T1.InvoiceId, T1.DocEntry, (Case when T1.InvType = '14' then (T1.SumApplied * -1) else T1.SumApplied end) as 'Sum Applied', T1.SumApplied, T1.AppliedFC, T1.InvType, (case when T1.InvType= '30' then 'JE' when T1.InvType = '13' then 'AR Inv' when T1.InvType = '14' then 'AR Inv' when T1.InvType = '24' then 'RC' else '' end) as 'DocType',  T1.DocLine FROM ORCT T0 INNER JOIN RCT2 T1 ON T0.DocEntry = T1.DocNum LEFT JOIN OINV T2 ON T2.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry and T1.InvType = T2.ObjType

LEFT JOIN ORIN T3 ON T3.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry and T1.InvType = T3.ObjType

LEFT JOIN OJDT T4 ON T4.TransId = T1.DocEntry and T1.InvType = T4.ObjType

WHERE T0.Canceled = 'N' and T0.DocDate between [%0] and [%1] and T2.DocDate <[%2]

 

 

Kedalene chong

entry.title: Re: How to validate manually entered batch numbers during...
entry.content:
Alternative approaches you can consider:

1) Number Range Control (Configuration)
Use external number ranges for batches and restrict manual entry to those ranges. This ensures that any manually entered batch number outside the defined range will be rejected by standard checks.

2) Validation via Custom Fields + Logic.
Instead of validating the batch number directly during creation, introduce a custom field (e.g., “Batch Validation Flag”) and implement validation logic in Custom Logic that checks your rules. You can then raise warnings or errors indirectly by controlling follow-on processes (e.g., goods movement posting).

3) Process Restriction
If strict validation is mandatory, consider disabling manual batch entry and enforce automatic batch number assignment with controlled number ranges. This avoids the need for manual validation altogether.

entry.title: Re: SAP Signavio - Object Centric View- Issue with Events are not appearing in Process Discovery
entry.content:
Hi Jai_Jain,

I've run into something similar while setting up OCPM in O2C. A few common causes worth checking, in case one applies to your setup:

  • Case scope filtering events out – If case scope was defined narrowly (e.g., tied to a specific object type or a date/status filter), any event that isn't linked to an object within that scope won't surface in discovery, even if the event itself is correctly modeled. Worth re-checking whether the scope excludes valid variants.
  • Object–event linkage in the semantic model – In OCPM, an event only shows up in discovery if it's correctly linked to at least one in-scope object via the relevant qualifier/role. If an event references an object type that wasn't fully mapped (or the foreign key/relationship wasn't captured during network discovery), it gets silently dropped rather than flagged as an error.
  • Thresholds/filters on the discovery view itself – Separate from data issues, Process Discovery often applies a default frequency or variant threshold. Low-frequency events can be hidden from the graph even though they exist in the underlying log. Worth resetting thresholds to zero to confirm this isn't just a display issue.
  • Source data completeness in Process Network Discovery – Since network discovery infers relationships automatically, it can miss weaker or indirect object relationships (e.g., events connected only through an intermediate object). If that link wasn't picked up, events tied only through that path won't appear.

My experience as to the fastest way to debug: grab the raw log for one of the missing events, check if it has a valid object reference, and see if that object type is actually inside your case scope. Hope that helps.

entry.title: Monthly Benefit Carry Forward with Hire Date Proration
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

While configuring a Reimbursement benefit with the frequency set to Monthly and Balance Carry Forward enabled, is there a way to prorate the Entitlement Amount only for the month in which the employee joins the organization, and have the benefit continue with the full monthly entitlement from the following month onward?

I am aware of the Benefits Exception feature. However, in organizations with frequent mid-month joiners, maintaining exceptions becomes labor-intensive and prone to errors, especially since the entitlement amount varies based on the employee's Pay Grade and Benefit Type.

I tried implementing this through a business rule, but it is prorating the Entitlement Amount for every month as long as the employee's hire date falls within the current benefit cycle (April to March).

For example, if an employee's monthly entitlement is ₹20,000 and they joined on 10 June 2026, the rule currently calculates the entitlement as ₹28,000 (₹14,000 for June + ₹14,000 for July). However, the expected entitlement should be ₹34,000, where ₹14,000 is the prorated entitlement for June and ₹20,000 is the full entitlement for July.

Has anyone implemented a similar requirement or found a standard approach to achieve this?

Thanks,
Mohd Saif

entry.title: Question regarding account number requirement in SAP payment processing
entry.content:
Hi Support Team,

I hope this email finds you well.

I am writing to confirm my understanding of the SAP payment process and to explain a Japan-specific payment method that may be affected. I would appreciate your confirmation on the following points.

First, is my understanding correct that if the account number is blank, the payment processing in SAP will not work properly?

Next, in Japan there is a payment method called "nofu" (納付), where you take a "nofusho" (納付書 / payment slip) to a government office, convenience store, or bank counter and pay in cash. "Nofu" is a payment method that does not require a bank account.

We post the payment journal entries for these "nofu" payments in SAP; however, if the account number is blank, that posting process does not work correctly.

Could you please advise how we should handle this case?

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,
Soma Arai

entry.title: Re: Teaching SAP Without Teaching SAP
entry.content:
Hi, Matt,

Thank you for this very valuable perspective. I really resonate with the idea that we shouldn’t start by asking, “How do we teach SAP?” but rather, “How does SAP help us better teach the concepts that students already need to understand?”

In my experience, especially in the areas of accounting, management information systems, and ERP systems, SAP becomes an extremely useful tool for transforming abstract concepts into concrete learning experiences. Students can see how a sales order affects delivery, invoicing, accounting, inventory, and management reporting. At that point, concepts such as integrated processes, traceability, internal control, segregation of duties, and data-driven decision-making are no longer just theoretical definitions.

I, too, believe that the main value lies not in memorizing SAP transactions, but in students understanding how a real organization works. When they work in an ERP system, they don’t just learn about business processes—they directly experience the connections between departments, data, documents, and decisions.

In my courses, SAP is used to bridge the gap between theory and practice: accounting, sales, procurement, production, projects, reporting, and automation. Students gain a better understanding of why data quality matters, why controls are necessary, why processes need to be standardized, and how technology supports the operations of modern organizations.

I would say that, often, students end up learning SAP precisely because they are trying to understand something else: a process, a business problem, a managerial decision, or a control situation. And this is probably the best justification for integrating SAP into the curriculum.

For me, SAP isn’t just a software platform taught to students, but an applied learning environment where concepts become visible, relevant, and connected to economic reality.

Thank you for starting this discussion. I think it’s a very important topic for all of us who are trying to bring university education closer to real-world practice in organizations.

 

Viorel BANTA

Bucharest University of Economic Studies, Romania

#SAP Academic Partnerships #CIG-IG #SAP

entry.title: Re: How to trigger automatic email notifications to custo...
entry.content:
Hi @Chetan_attal ,

Short answer: There is no standard, out-of-the-box feature in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition that sends a payment-confirmation email to the customer when an incoming payment is posted, and Output Management cannot do it either. The supported way to build this is event-driven: consume the Journal Entry business event and send the email from SAP Integration Suite.

1. Is there a standard, out-of-the-box configuration to email the customer when a payment is posted?

No. Incoming payments (on-account, advance/down payment, or invoice clearing) all post as accounting/journal entry documents, and there is no standard app or configuration that generates an outbound customer email on that posting. The standard payment-communication objects in the product — payment advices and payment media — belong to outgoing Accounts Payable payments to suppliers, not to incoming customer payments, so they don't cover your requirement. Reference: Feature Scope Description 2602.

2. Can this be achieved with SAP S/4HANA Output Management (via the payment/clearing document)?

No. Output Control in Public Cloud supports the channels PRINT, EMAIL and EDI, but only for the business objects that are output-enabled (e.g. billing documents/customer invoices, sales orders, purchase orders, outbound deliveries, dunning). The FI journal entry / clearing document produced by an incoming payment is not an output-management-enabled object, so there is no output type or determination you can configure against it. Reference: Output Parameter Determination, Working with Output Items.

3. Recommended workaround

Recommended: Business Events + SAP Integration Suite. This is the clean, upgrade-safe pattern in Public Cloud. Note it requires significant effort to be established.

Publish the event: every posted payment creates a Journal Entry, which raises a standard Journal Entry business event. You expose it through Enterprise Event Enablement — set up a Communication Arrangement for scenario SAP_COM_0092 and create the outbound topic binding in Enterprise Event Enablement – Configure Channel Binding. Reference: Business Event Handling, Journal Entry events on Business Accelerator Hub.

Consume and send: route the event to SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration). Note the messaging layer options — the standalone SAP Event Mesh is being phased out for new (BTPEA) contracts; use Event Mesh for Integration Suite (EMIS), which is included, or Advanced Event Mesh if you need higher scale. Reference: Advanced Event Mesh.

Important: the business event payload delivers only document keys (company code, fiscal year, accounting document), not the amount, currency, or customer email. Your iFlow therefore needs to (a) call the Journal Entry API to read amount + currency, (b) read the customer's email from Business Partner master data, then (c) send the mail via the Mail adapter with your dynamic text.

Filter: a Journal Entry event fires for all FI postings, so filter in the iFlow (e.g. by document/transaction type or clearing indicator) to isolate genuine incoming customer payments and avoid emailing on unrelated postings.

Other suggested options from your text:

  • On a custom BAdI: possible but not advisable here. Released FI posting BAdIs run in the ABAP Cloud sandbox and are not intended to fire external customer emails synchronously during posting; keeping the logic in Integration Suite is cleaner, decoupled, and easier to maintain.
  • On Situation Handling: not suitable, because it notifies internal SAP business users only and cannot target external, non-SAP customers.

Summary

No standard feature and no Output Management route exists for this. Build it event-driven: publish the Journal Entry event via Enterprise Event Enablement (SAP_COM_0092), consume it in SAP Integration Suite (EMIS or Advanced Event Mesh), enrich the amount/currency and the customer email via APIs, filter for real incoming payments, and send through the Mail adapter. Confirm the exact event payload on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub for release 2602 before you design the iFlow.

Hope it helps.
Best regards,
Michal

entry.title: Re: Request for Maintenance Certificate for Development S...
entry.content:
I understand your could not generate maintenance key for S/4HANA system 850450600.

I can see you have a valid SAP Rise contract; however, the blocker is that there is no active maintenance contract for installation 21167223. It also appears that maintenance coverage for all your on-premise installations has expired. 

Regarding this issue, you may refer to KBA 2436412 - License Keys application shows message: You do not have a maintenance contract for this installation - SAP for Me

To move this forward, please contact your SAP Account Manager or SAP Order Management to review and update the maintenance coverage on your account:

My product support team can only tell you if you could select the related license material to generate or renew the key, we can't fix issues like this. Therefore, please work directly with SAP Order Management (contracts) and/or your SAP Account Manager to check your account and make any necessary updates. After your account is updated and authorized for the product, you will be able to use it.

If you still have such issue after contacting with them, please do not hesitate to contact with us again.

Thanks for your understanding and cooperation.

Hi @katoszilard,

the list above is complete in regards to what was removed / changed.
We have decided not to stop support for older themes, because we gain little and cause a lot of frustration for users. However, this does not mean that old themes get any new features: Signature will never look good on high resolution displays or support multi monitor scaling for example.

So, to answer your question: No themes have been removed in 8.10.

Best regards,
Frank

entry.title: Clearing G/L Line Items Using Processing Rules in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
In today's fast-paced business environment, efficiency and accuracy in financial management are paramount. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition offers powerful automated processing rules that simplify and expedite the bank statement reconciliation process, ensuring financial data is accurate and up to date. This blog walks you through how to automatically clear G/L line items in payment documents against bank items using Processing Rules - a capability that significantly reduces manual effort and minimizes the risk of human error.

Note: All images and data referenced in this blog are from SAP internal systems, sample data, or demo systems. Any resemblance to real data is purely coincidental.

Finance teams often face the challenge of manually matching and clearing G/L line items in payment documents against corresponding bank items. Without automation, this process is time-consuming and error-prone, especially in high-volume transaction environments. The key question is: How can G/L line items in a payment document be automatically cleared against the bank item using Processing Rules in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?

When bank statements are imported, the system initially attempts to match open items using configured posting rules and interpretation algorithms. However, if the system fails to match an incoming payment with an open item using predefined posting rules, the remaining bank statement information is checked against custom processing rules. 

Solution: Step-by-Step Configuration Guide
Understanding the Two Core Concepts Before You Begin

Before diving into the steps, it is important to understand the distinction between Posting Rules and Processing Rules in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

Posting Rules are predefined and preconfigured by SAP and cannot be altered by customers. They are standardized to ensure consistency and compliance.
Processing Rules are fully customizable by customers to meet their specific business needs. This distinction allows for a balance between maintaining system integrity and providing customization options.

Step 1: Open the Manage Processing Rules App

  • Log into your SAP Fiori Launchpad and search for the Manage Processing Rules – For Bank Statements app (also referred to as Manage Bank Statement Reprocessing Rules).
  • This app is where you define the conditions and actions that processing rules will perform on bank statement items.

Step 2: Create a New Processing Rule

  • Click the Create button to open the rule editor.
  • On the Processing Rule tab, fill in the following header information:
    • Description: Enter a clear, descriptive name for the rule (e.g., "Auto-Clear G/L Items – Incoming Payments").
    • Journal Entry Type: Select the appropriate document type (e.g., DZ – Customer Payment).
    • Rule for: Select Incoming Payments or Outgoing Payments as applicable.
    • Action Type: Choose the relevant action type. For clearing G/L line items, select Clear in Sequence – Activates interpretation and allocation algorithms to clear open items.

Step 3: Define Conditions

  • Navigate to the Conditions tab. This is where the rule "matches" the correct bank statement items. [4]
  • Add one or more conditions using the following attributes:
    • Attributes: Categories of data to filter on - examples include Amount, Company Code, Currency, External Transaction, House Bank, Memo Line, Partner IBAN, Partner Name, etc.
    • Operators: Define the type of comparison - examples include Equals, Contains, Between, Greater Than, etc.
    • From/To: Define the value to search for. 
  • Ensure conditions are specific enough so the rule only triggers for the right transactions. It is also recommended to include House Bank and Account ID conditions to restrict the rule to a specific bank account.

Step 4: Configure the Action (Based on Action Type)

Depending on the Action Type selected in Step 2, configure the corresponding action details: 

  • For Clear in Sequence: Choose the Processing Instruction MTCH that dictate how the system locates the open items.
     
    • Configure the Processing Instruction to define how the system searches memo line data against open items.
    • Select the Source Field from the dropdown list. The Source Field field refers to a field in the payment advice item or bank statement item (and also in the bank statement item's subitem), for example, the Journal Entry (Advice) or the Assignment Reference (Bank Statement) field. By determining the source field, you tell the system to use the value in that specific field of the payment advice item or bank statement item, and to look for a matching value in the field of an open item.
    • Select the Use Source Field As from the dropdown list. The Use Source Field as field refers to a field of an open item, for example, the Journal Entry (BELNR), the Reference (XBLNR), or Reference Key (XREF) field of open items. By determining this field, you tell the system to look for matching values in this field.



Note: If you select the Match Date checkbox, the system also compares the dates of the payment advice item and of the open items. If you deselect the Match Date checkbox, the system does not compare any dates, and simply uses the values defined in the Source Field and Use Source Field as fields in order to match items.

Restriction
If you select bank-statement-related fields as the source field in the sequence, you cannot select the Match Date checkbox. You can only select the Match Date checkbox with payment-advice-related fields.

If you select the Intercompany Open Items checkbox, the system also includes intercompany open items in the search for matching open items with the defined values for the Source Field and Use Source Field as fields. If you deselect the Intercompany Open Items checkbox, the system does not include any intercompany open items, and only searches for open items belonging to the company code of the bank statement item.

The sequence that you define here is submitted to the allocation algorithm that is determined in the selected processing instruction. The allocation algorithm then uses this sequence to compare the values in specific fields in payment advice items or bank statement items and open items, and thus, to identify matching items which can then be cleared.

Example

In the Clear in Sequence section, you have selected a processing instruction that contains an allocation algorithm with the correct configurations for payment advice processing, such as the MTCH processing instruction. In the Match Items in Sequence section, you define the following values:


Row Number

Source Field

Use Source Field as

1

Memo Line (Bank Statement Item)

Journal Entry

2

Customer Reference Number (Bank Statement Item)

Reference

The system reads this sequence as follows: First, take the value in the Memo Line field of the bank statement item, and compare it with the value in the Journal Entry field of the open items. If the values match, the bank statement item and open item are matched. If not, proceed with the second row: Take the value in the Customer Reference Number (Bank Statement Item) field of the bank statement item, and compare it with the value in the Reference field of the open items. If the values match, the bank statement item and open item are matched. If not, continue with the next processing instruction, or display the item in the Reprocess Bank Statement Items app for manual processing.

  • Under Open Item type section, select G/L Items. The default value of this field is AP/AR Items. If you want to, you can determine that you want the processing instruction to look for G/L items. If you do select G/L Items, you need to specify a range of G/L accounts that the processing instruction should search to find matching open G/L items to be cleared.
  • Define residual item handling, reason codes, or on-account fallback options as needed. 
  • Click on Apply.

Step 5: Save the Rule

  • Click Create to save the rule. 
  • The rule is now saved and available for manual use in the Reprocess Bank Statement Items app.

Step 6: Automate the Rule

To have the rule apply automatically during bank statement import - without manual intervention - you must activate automation: 

  • Select the saved rule in the list view.
  • Click the Automate Rule button (available in the top-right or via the action menu).
  • In the pop-up dialog, select the Procedure Type:
    • 001 – One-Step Posting: Only Posting Area 1 (PA1 – Bank Accounting) will be posted. The Bank Accounting journal entry is posted to the G/L account mentioned in the processing rule and the main bank G/L account. 
    • 002 – Two-Step Posting: Both PA1 (Bank Accounting) and PA2 (Subledger Accounting) will be posted. The Bank Accounting journal entry posts to the main bank G/L account and the bank subaccount; the Subledger Accounting journal entry posts to the bank subaccount and the AP/AR account.
  • Confirm by clicking Automate Rule.

The rule is now active and will be applied automatically for the next incoming bank statement imports.

Step 7: Verify Processing via the Reprocess Bank Statement Items App

After the next bank statement is imported, verify that the rule was applied correctly:

  • Open the Reprocess Bank Statement Items app.
  • Locate the relevant bank statement item and check the processing log.
  • The log will confirm whether the rule was applied and the journal entries were posted successfully.
  • If no open item is found for a bank statement item, the item will need to be handled manually in this app.

Important Note: Handling Multiple Matching Rules

If multiple processing rules match the same bank statement item, the system stops the application of the processing rule and raises the following error:

"Bnk stmnt X itm Y matches conditions of several rules. No rule applied."

In this case, the normal posting rule assigned to the external transaction code in the SSCUI will be applied instead. To avoid this, ensure your rule conditions are specific and non-overlapping. 

Conclusion

Automating the clearing of G/L line items using Processing Rules in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is a highly effective way to improve efficiency and accuracy in financial close and bank reconciliation processes. By configuring the Manage Processing Rules – For Bank Statements app with the right conditions, action types, and procedure types, finance teams can significantly reduce manual workload and ensure consistent, accurate postings. [1]

Key takeaways:

  • Posting Rules are SAP-delivered and fixed; Processing Rules are customer-configurable.
  • Use Action Type: Clear in Sequence with the processing instruction MTCH to automatically clear G/L open items.
  • Always activate automation on the rule to ensure it runs during bank statement import.
  • Monitor results via the Reprocess Bank Statement Items app and handle exceptions manually where needed. 

For further reading, refer to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud documentation on Automatic Bank Statement Processing and KBA 3780076 on SAP for Me.

entry.title: Re: Reject Creating Outbound Delivery
entry.content:
Hello @mrbeam 

What is the business rationale behind that requirement? Why would you want to prevent delivery creation in EWM?

One very dirty method could be to throw an abort message / short dump in the inbound mapping BAdI. But again why?

Such rejections are surefire recipe for a disaster, for ERP-EWM inconsistencies - deliveries created in ERP will never be created in EWM; they will just stick forgotten for ever in qRFC queues. Typically EWM implementation struggle with the opposite challenge - to keep ERP and EWM consistent and minimize outstanding qRFC entries.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: Screen Painter Blank in SAPMV45A screen
entry.content:
The note 2297134 is not about "blank dynpro", it's about regenerating the "dynpro load" ; your issue is about the "dynpro source".

See in the Version Management why and who cleared the dynpro layout, and see in the transport request if other objects are impacted.

Solution : restore the previous version or import the dynpro from another system via the Transport Management System.

Contact SAP Support if you need more assistance.

entry.title: Applicability of Alternative FYV in a 2SL Environment
entry.content:
We are currently planning the implementation of new company codes and have encountered questions regarding the Fiscal Year Variant (FYV) setup. We would be grateful if you could clarify the following points.

Current Situation and Considerations:

We are exploring the possibility of assigning a different Fiscal Year Variant (Alternative FYV) to specific new company codes, distinct from the FYV used by our existing company codes.

Our understanding is that this Alternative FYV must be assigned to a Non-Leading Ledger (e.g., 2L, 3L etc.), and not to the Leading Ledger (0L). Could you please confirm if this understanding is correct?

Our current system operates in a 2SL environment.

Questions:

Applicability of Alternative FYV in a 2SL Environment:

    • In our current 2SL environment, is it feasible to assign an Alternative FYV to a Non-Leading Ledger for a new company code?
    • We would like to clarify if a 3SL configuration is also a prerequisite for assigning an Alternative FYV to a Non-Leading Ledger when introducing a new company code.
    • <

entry.title: Re: Unable to Set Payment Terms and Payment Method for Ve...
entry.content:
Dear @StrOk,

The posted result is standard system behavior. In the journal entry the residual supplier item has Is Negative Posting = Yes and Debit/Credit Code = S, confirming that it was created as a negative debit posting. Although displayed as a negative amount in the Debit column, its balance effect corresponds to a credit posting

There is no residual-item-level option in Clear Outgoing Payments (F1367) to force the item to be posted as a positive amount in the Credit column. The residual amount should retain the same sign as the underlying supplier item and should not be reversed only to change its presentation

Negative postings are controlled more broadly at company-code and document-type level. Changing this configuration would affect other postings and is not recommended solely to change the display of this residual item. No correction is required for the posted item

More details below:

SAP Help – Negative Postings

SAP KBA 3157729 – General Journal Entries Negative Postings SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Add a Standalone Selling Price (SSP) as a Pricing Condition in SAP Subscription Billing
entry.content:
What You'll Build 

  • A priceelement specification (e.g., 'Recurring SSP') which needs to be assigned to the PSAM condition type. 
  • A lookup tablewith an entry of the SSP amount per product/market/rate element (maintainable via Excel import or directly in the UI). 
  • thecreated price element specification needs to be added as a new price element to the pricing scheme (e.g., step 300) defined as a statistical price element. 
  • An end-to-end verification:simulate inSB (seats/quantity), then create a Solution Order and confirm the statistical SSP appears in the revenue contract. 

Why 'statistical'? The SSP should not change the customer price; it is used as a basis for revenue allocation (CBRR/URR), so you define it as statistical in SB. 

Understand Pricing in SB 

If you are already very familiar with pricing definition in SB, you can skip this section.  

 In Subscription Billing, a product can have multiple rate plans assigned. Each rate plan can consist of a different pricing scheme, different billing cycle, including different price element specifications. 

 

Let’s have a look at the above example 

You can start with a single product—let’s call it “Cloud Subscription.” 

You can attach multiple rate plans to it. In our example, you’ll add three: 

  • Rate Plan 1: Yearly Settlement – billed once per year 
  • Rate Plan 2: Quarterly Settlement – billed once per quarter 
  • Rate Plan 3: Monthly Settlement – billed monthly 

With one product and multiple rate plans, you can support different pricing and different billing cycles side by side—ideal when customers want the same service but prefer different payment cadences. 

Next, you’ll decide how each rate plan calculates the price. That’s the job of the pricing scheme. 

  • For Yearly and Quarterly, you can assign the same Pricing Scheme, e.g., “Pricing with Discount”. You can offer an incentive (like a recurring discount) for customers who commit to longer billing cycles. 
  • For Monthly, you can assign the Pricing Scheme, e.g., “Monthly Pricing”. Here, you keep it simple: a flat monthly fee and no recurring discount. 

A pricing scheme is built of price element specifications—your reusable building blocks that define what the scheme charges and how it calculates each price element. 

In this example, it is setup like the following: 

Price Scheme A: Pricing with Discount (used by Yearly + Quarterly) 

  • OneTime Charge – to cover setup or onboarding 
  • Recurring Charge – the base subscription fee 
  • Recurring Discount – the incentive for longer commitment 

Price Scheme B: Monthly Pricing (used by Monthly), no discount is given. 

  • OneTime Charge – optional; include it if your monthly plan needs it 
  • Recurring Charge – a flat monthly price 

Now for the revenue recognition friendly part: you can add a Standalone Selling Price (SSP) as a statistical price element to your pricing scheme. This line: 

  • Appears in pricing only for statistical purpose, 
  • Does not change what the customer pays (because it’s statistical), and 
  • Feeds downstream revenue processes for allocation and compliance. 

You can reuse the same “SSP” price element specification in both schemes: 

  • Scheme A (Pricing with Discount) → add SSP (statistical) 
  • Scheme B (Monthly Pricing) → add SSP (statistical) 

Now as you have understood the high-level picture, let’s have a detailed look how this is configured. 

Step-by-Step Guidance 

Step 1 — Create (or Update) the Lookup Table (Your SSP Records) 

Now you will create the lookup table in order to define the SSP amount, using app ‘Manage Lookup Tables’ 

One convenient way is to copy from a similar lookup table which is already available, such as Recurring Pricing. 

Then you will define a lookup table name, for example “SSP”. As input parameters we will use in this example: Product Code, Market ID, and Rate Element (e.g., Recurring). For the output parameter, we will use price. 

The lookup table structure is defined, and now you need to maintain the data entries in the lookup table.  

To do so, you can click the view data button when you review the lookup table  

 

Or you can use Apps Manage Lookup Table Data. 

You either need to export the table as an excel file, maintain your pricing, and import the file to update the data or you can easily provide data directly in the user interface by clicking on create button for a new entry. 

Step 2 — Create a Price Element Specification for SSP 

Open Manage Price Element Specification.

You can copy the existing specification such as  'Recurring Price' specification as a reference to save time. You can of course create a new one from scratch.

Define the name, e.g.'Recurring SSP'. 

In the pricing tree section of the price element specification, you define how the price will be determined. In the following, we select the newly created lookup table “SSP” for retrieving the SSP entry from our lookup table. 

 

In the section of Custom Output Attributes, we set the Condition Type to PSAM. This is essential for Revenue Accounting recognition as it recognizes it as the standalone selling price. 

For other settings, such as rounding, referencing data, you can use the one from the template or adjust it to your own needs. 

Step 3 — Add the Price Element Specification for SSP to Your Pricing Scheme 

Open the Pricing Scheme used by your product’s rate plan. You can easily navigate from the product app to the respective pricing scheme (see below).

Add a new step (for example, 300) to the pricing scheme. Then select the price element specification 'Recurring SSP' from step 2. 

Define the step name (e.g., 'Recurring SSP'),  and take as the Sign = '+', and mark the step as Statistical so it does not affect the net price but is available for allocation in Revenue Accounting. 

 

Step 4 — Verify in Subscription Billing (Quick Simulation) 

Open your Product, ensure the Rate Plan uses your updated Pricing Scheme 

You can create a subscription with the product and select the rate plan.  

Once you give the correct parameters and click the apply button, you should see the price for the recurring charge and the statistical SSP price element.  

Step 5 – Validation the SSP condition type is part of the pricing procedure of the solution order. 

A subscription is usually created from a solution order as a follow up document. We have  defined PSAM as the condition type for the SSP price element in subscription billing. Important to consider is that the condition type needs to be part of the solution order pricing procedure as well.  

In CE2602, PSAM has been added to the standard pricing procedure of solution order.  You can validate in Manage Your Solution-> Pricing->Set Pricing Procedures 

Note: However, if you have already copied the standard pricing procedure to your own pricing procedure such as Z1024, and you are using it, then you need make sure PSAM is added also to your already copied pricing procedure. And you take the standard pricing procedure such as A10024 as a reference. 

Step 6 — Check it from End-to-End: Solution Order to Revenue Contract 

Create a Solution Order and add the Subscription product with the correct rate plan and parameters (e.g., seats). Simulate your subscription by clicking on the simulate button on the Subscription Line Item level to confirm that the SSP line appears as statistical in the price calculation section. 

After that release the items and open the Revenue Contract (or Managed Revenue Accounting Items).  

 

Confirm that the amount SSP matches your lookup table resolution. 

 

Quick Checklist 

☐ Price element specification created ('Recurring SSP') 

☐ Price element specification is assigned to Condition type PSAM. 

☐ Lookup table populated and active (Product/Market/Rate Element → Price). 

☐ Pricing scheme updated (e.g., Step 300), Sign = '+', Statistical = Yes 

☐Simulation shows SSP price element with respective value 

☐PSAM is included in the pricing procedure of Solution Order.  

☐ Revenue Contract reflects statistical SSP after Solution Order release.

entry.title: Ariba Next Gen: Unlocking Flexibility with Extensibility Hub
entry.content:
As organizations evolve, so do their business requirements. Standard application capabilities often need to be enhanced to meet specific operational needs. This is where the Extensibility Hub in Ariba Next Gen plays a crucial role—empowering administrators to customize and extend application functionality with ease.

🔍What is Extensibility Hub:  A shared service framework that allows administrators to create and manage custom extensions. These extensions can be published across multiple Ariba modules, including: Sourcing, Contracts, Buying, invoicing etc.

- Unlimited Custom Fields No restriction on the number of custom fields you can create
- Cross-Application Reusability Reuse fields across multiple modules effortlessly            
- Expression Support Build business logic without affecting standard functionality
- Synchronization (Sync) Align and reuse custom fields across project
- Version History Tracking Easily track project changes over time
- Agent Support Extend functionality further with agents
- Business Usage Extend fields across modules and expose them to CIG
- Custom field should get auto replicated to S4 Hana public cloud

 

⚠️Before you start, keep these key points in mind:

- Expression cannot be tested directly in Extensibility Hub
- Must be validated via UI adoption and navigation
-️ Technical Naming Rules Field technical names must NOT contain spaces
- Publishing Time Publishing custom fields takes approximately 7–8 minutes

🛠️ How to Configure follow these simple steps to get started:

Navigate to Extensibility Hub:  Admin → Global Settings → Extensibility (this will land admin user to the extensibility hub)

 

Create an Extensibility Project, Start a new project

Project is a collection of entities and extensions (both regular and customized) created for an application. It serves as the container for managing and organizing extensions, allowing grouping related entities together for easier deployment

project is a collection of entities and extensions (both regular and customized) created for an application. It serves as the container for managing and organizing extensions, allowing grouping related entities together for easier deployment

Define Field Placement, create field and add expression under view content

choose header or line item to add the field

 technical name should be without space

 Write expression under view content ✅ Align logic with business needs

Publish Custom Fields

🚀Publish your fields ⏱️ Allow ~7–8 minutes for deployment (Click publish to make fields available to Adopt on UI)

 

Adopt Fields in UI

- Enable via UI adoption process

- Validate through navigation before making visible

Click on Adopt UI to make field available on UI

 
 
 

Click on navigation to test the expression before you make fields available for end users

🏁Final Thoughts

The Extensibility Hub in Ariba Next Gen provides a powerful yet controlled way to enhance applications based on business needs. By using it effectively, organizations can achieve the perfect balance between Standardization and Customization

👉Resulting in both stability and agility across business processes.

entry.title: Transfer Posting using Synchronous Goods Movement in EWM
entry.content:
Introduction:

In SAP EWM, almost all physical goods movements are processed through deliveries. While this approach provides end-to-end traceability and precise inventory tracking, it can at times become time-consuming and cumbersome and especially when issues arise that require immediate resolution.

In many cases, users also feel that not every goods movement requires delivery-based processing. To address these scenarios and simplify operational processes, SAP has Synchronous Goods Movement functionality in EWM. This feature enables posting of goods movements directly in MIGO without creating deliveries, reducing process complexity while still ensuring accurate inventory updates.

In today’s blog we will see how to  carry out transfer posting between storage locations with Synchronous Goods Movement in SAP EWM. But before we dive into the business process let us take a look at the configuration and setup to enable this functionality.

Configuration:

Step 1: Go to SPRO -> SCM EWM -> EWM -> Interfaces -> ERP Integration -> Goods Movement -> Activate Synchronous Goods Movement Posting.

Step 2: Go to SPRO -> SCM EWM -> EWM -> Interfaces -> ERP Integration -> Goods Movement -> Settings for Synchronous Goods Movements (MIGO) -> Activate Synchronous Goods Movements in the Warehouse.

Here we have three options.

Transfer Posting: Activating this for movements like 301, 311.

Goods Receipt: You can perform any goods receipt related movement type, such as 101, 561, 501, etc.

Goods Issue: For movements like 201, 261, etc

Note: For transferring from EWM to MM, or for posting a goods issue from EWM, make sure that the "No Goods Issue" field is unchecked for the storage type.

Testing:

Scenario 1: Transfer Posting from EWM to IM Storage Location (IMSL):

Go to transaction MIGO and select Transfer Posting -> Others, and here I am choosing the movement type as 311, which is Transfer Posting within storage location.

I am transferring the material from an EWM-managed storage location to an IM-managed storage location (IMSL).

Step 1: Enter the material, plant, storage location in Transfer posting tab and quantity in the Quantity tab

Step 2: In the warehouse management tab give the Storage bin where the stock lies.

Step 3: Click on post. Here instead of delivery generation, we get the material document posted and the warehouse task is generated in the background automatically.

Warehouse Document:

Stock Before Posting:

Stock After Posting:

Scenario 2: Transfer Posting from IMSL to EWM:

In this scenario, I am transferring the material from an IM-managed storage location to an EWM-managed storage location.

Step 1: Enter the Material, storage location in the Transfer Posting tab.

Step 2: Enter the quantity in the Quantity tab.

Step 3: In the Warehouse Management tab, enter the storage bin and select by quantity per HU, to select the packaging material and number of HU required.

Step 4: Now Post the document. Once it’s posted the material document is generated indicating the goods are received in the warehouse. The warehouse task is created in the background with the relevant HU.

Warehouse Document:

Stock Before Posting:

Stock After Posting:

Conclusion:

As shown above, Synchronous Goods Movement lets you post simple transfer postings, goods receipts, and goods issues directly from MIGO, eliminating the need to generate an EWM delivery for every movement. This is particularly useful for quick, ad-hoc stock corrections or transfers where full delivery-based traceability is not required, helping reduce process overhead while keeping EWM and ERP stock in sync. As always, evaluate this against your traceability and reporting requirements before enabling it broadly in a productive warehouse.

entry.title: Decoupling Unload and GR with POSC setup where TU is used for SAP EWM Inbound operations
entry.content:
This Blog post is intended to provider reader  how to decouple Unload and GR with POSC setup where TU is used for Inbound operations 

Business Scenario –

For one of our Automotive customers the Yard operator uses their third-party application which does all the transportation planning and EWM is expected to follow the Check in , Movement to Parking lot/Door, unloading, check out at the TU level as per events received from Third party. All the physical movements done with third party applications had to be in sync with TU status and business should be able to track exact TU status for Check in, Dock to door, Unloading etc. in SAP.

There was an ask to Decouple the Unloading and GR in SAP where-in we had Complex Unloading setup with steps for Unloading, Deconsolidate and Put away.

As per standard SAP setup, the Unloading Warehouse task Confirmation post Implicit Good receipt in the background however business ask was to decouple the same behavior.

We could achieve this setup by discarding Unloading WT creation step in the POSC and changing the Goods movement setup in SAP to Delivery level. Please go through the below details to understand current behavior and solution followed.

Current setup Behavior -

As per the screen capture, the TU is docked to the door and waiting for Unloading to be done. 

SAP copies Warehouse door from TU to IBD and same can be seen in the below screen shot. As per these values ,  System creates Unloading task from TU to ‘Staging Bay’

Next when We get event from third party Application that Unloading is finished, EWM Unloading task execution happens in the background and TU status gets updated with Unloading start/Finish. Once we set this Unloading finish at the TU system confirms unloading task in the background and simultaneously posts GR as well.

As per below screenshot , the unloading task is confirmed when we select ‘ Unload finished’ at the TU level and 2nd warehouse is created to move Nested HU with 2 products to Deconsolidation area.

Nested HU with 2 different products.

 

New Setup Behaviour-

Firstly, Configuration for Goods movement control must be set to ‘1. Goods movement to Storage Bin Specified in Delivery’.

New POSC is created with steps for Deconsolidation and Put away (Deleted Step IB01 for unloading)

Removed door from Staging area and door determination otherwise Goods movement bin is copied as door bin in the replicated IBD in EWM.

The Door bin is removed from  assignment of Door to SCU and storage bin.

Now as we can see in the Screen shot below, Staging bin is copied as ‘ Goods movement bin’

Later when TU is docked to door, the Door is copied from TU to IBD, but Goods movement bin is not updated and remains ‘ Staging bin’

As per TU status, Arrival at check point and docking to door is finished and now TU is ready to execute unloading.

As per our setup, we get unloading start and finish events from third part at this point of time and we set TU status accordingly.

To simulate above posting, execution is done in the RF as below.

Execution by RFUI menu--> Rec HU by ASN

 

 

As we can see the unloading , GR and WT creation status are all blanks.

Yard operator selects push button – ‘ UnLo’ and Unloading status is set to done.

The Unloading status is reflected in the in TU and most importantly the GR status remains open.

IBD shows ‘Unloading’ Completed but ‘Good receipt’ not started.

Next truck will move back to check point. TU departs from warehouse, and TU status is set to completed.

Now Logistic operators can do GR and rest of the warehouse operations at their own convenience . System’s only prerequisite to execute other warehouse operations with RF is to finish Unloading which is already done.

To execute GR, Logistic operator selects same RF menu -->Rec HU by ASN.

Unloading is already done so he will select GR and WT to move the pallet to Deconsolidation is created in the background.

 

Good receipt status is set to complete and Only Deconsolidation warehouse task is created to move the Pallet from Staging bin to Deconsolidation workstation. Unloading tasks are not created as per changed setup.

Conclusion –

Decoupling Unload and GR with POSC setup where TU is used for Inbound operations can be achieved by disregarding Unloading warehouse steps from POSC and setting up goods movement to TU.

This approach enables us to handle Missing pallets/extra pallets in the IBD with very ease for warehouse employees. Reversing GR and ‘reverse unloading ( without WT ) can be executed at IBD level.

Thanks for reading the blog, I hope that this can be beneficial.

Thank you,

Harish Patil

entry.title: Freight Order Quantity Analysis
entry.content:
In SAP Transportation Management in transportation execution and operational visibility scenarios. In one weekly review, the business team wanted to understand whether transportation volume was evenly distributed across carriers.

The first comparison was based mainly on the number of completed Freight Orders. At first glance, two carriers looked almost equal because their Freight Order counts were close. However, when we reviewed the transported gross weight and gross volume, we found that the actual workload was not the same.

For confidentiality, I have used representative carrier names and adjusted figures. The scenario is based on the type of operational review I have worked on, but the numbers are not production values.
Accessing the App the Freight Order Quantity Analysis app can be accessed from the SAP Fiori launchpad.

  1. Open the SAP Fiori launchpad.
  2. Search for Freight Order Quantity Analysis.
  3. Open the app with Fiori app ID F2676.
  4. From the overview page, select the relevant analytical card or data point.
  5. Navigate to the detailed view to analyze the Freight Orders behind the selected KPI.

The overview page provides different analytical cards, such as gross weight, gross volume, quantity distribution, carrier quantity analysis, shipper quantity analysis, ship to party quantity analysis, transportation mode and utilization.


Figure 1: Freight Order Quantity Analysis overview page - Fiori app F2676

Filters Used for the Analysis from the overview page, I selected the Carrier Quantity Analysis card and opened the detailed view. For the analysis, I used the following filter and display selections:

  • Transportation Mode Category: Road
  • Destination Country/Region: United States
  • View By: Carrier
  • Measure: Gross Weight
  • Display Weight Unit: Metric Tons
  • Display Volume Unit: Cubic Metres
  • Display Quantity Unit: Each
  • Completion Period: Selected completion weeks within the available current and previous month period

The standard app focuses on completed Freight Orders for the current and previous month. Because of this, I used the Completion Year/Week field to compare weekly movement trends.

These fields helped to compare the number of Freight Orders against the actual transportation volume.


Figure 2: Detailed view with carrier, transportation mode, units and completion week filters

Representative Carrier Comparison, The original business question was simple: Which carrier handled more work this week?
If we looked only at the number of Freight Orders, Carrier A appeared to have handled slightly more work than Carrier B.

Measure

Carrier A

Carrier B

Completed Freight Orders

42

39

Gross Weight

840 metric tons

1,260 metric tons

Gross Volume

1,960 m³

2,450 m³

Based on Freight Order count alone, Carrier A looked slightly higher because it completed 42 Freight Orders compared with Carrier B’s 39 Freight Orders. However, the quantity analysis showed a different story. Carrier B completed three fewer Freight Orders, but transported approximately 50% more gross weight. Carrier B also transported higher gross volume.
This finding changed the discussion. Carrier A had more documents, but Carrier B had a higher actual transportation workload. Carrier B was handling fewer but heavier movements, while Carrier A was handling a larger number of smaller shipments.

The main finding was that Freight Order count should not be used as the only measure of carrier workload. In this example, the business initially assumed that Carrier A had a slightly higher workload because it had more completed Freight Orders. After reviewing gross weight and gross volume, we could see that Carrier B had consumed more transportation capacity.
This was useful for three reasons:
1. It gave the team a better view of actual carrier workload.
2. It supported a more meaningful capacity discussion with carriers.
3. It helped explain why carrier utilization may look different from the document count.

The app also helped identify weekly movement patterns. When I changed the view to Completion Week, I could see the week in which Carrier B’s transported weight increased. This made it easier to review the underlying Freight Orders and understand what caused the change. Drilling Down into the Freight Orders after identifying the increase for Carrier B, I reviewed the detailed table and checked the Freight Orders behind the result.
Compared the following information:

  • Source and destination locations
  • Gross weight and volume
  • Number of containers
  • Transportation mode
  • Freight Order completion date
  • Carrier assignment

The list showed that Carrier B had handled several high weight movements to the same destination region during that week. This explained why the gross weight was higher even though the Freight Order count was lower. This is the part I found most useful. The overview helped identify the trend and the detailed view helped explain the reason behind it.


Figure 3: Carrier comparison showing Freight Order count, gross weight, and gross volume

It is also important to understand that this analysis is based on completed Freight Orders. Freight Orders that are still being planned or executed may not be represented in the same way. This can explain differences between the application and another operational report that includes planned or in execution documents.
From a technical perspective, the app uses the C_FrtOrdQuantityQuery CDS view. The C_FRTORDQUANTITYQUERY_CDS OData service must be activated in the SAP Gateway system. Users also need the required SAP Transportation Management and SAP Fiori authorizations to access the app and view the analytical data. No custom development was required for this analysis. The key requirements were correct Freight Order data, the required authorizations and activation of the supporting analytical service.

Conclusion
The Freight Order Quantity Analysis app gives better insight into transportation volumes and supports more informed decision making. It helps teams understand not just how many Freight Orders are moving, but also how much business volume is being executed. The app may look simple at first, but when used together with operational knowledge, it can provide useful insights that are not always visible from standard Freight Order reports.

Reference: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/612fbe25aed64068a7aca8e8e8339de1.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com

entry.title: SAP Document AI Trial Booster Fails at "Subscribing SaaS Applications"
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

 
I am trying to set up SAP Document AI Trial in an SAP BTP Trial subaccount using the "Set Up Account for SAP Document AI" Booster.
 
Environment:
- SAP BTP Trial Account
- Region: US10
- Subaccount: 9e0da020trial
- Runtime: Cloud Foundry
 
Booster Result:
The booster consistently completes the following steps successfully:
✓ Assigning Service Quotas
✓ Creating Service Instances
 
However, it fails at:
 
✗ Subscribing SaaS Applications
 
As a result:
 
↷ Assigning Role Collections is skipped
 
Current Status:
 
The following service instance was created successfully:
 
Service: SAP Document AI Trial
Technical Name: document-information-extraction-trial
Instance: default_document-information-extraction-trial
Plan: blocks_of_100
 
Available Role Templates:
 
Under Security → Roles, I can see the following Document AI role templates:
 
- Document_Information_Extraction_UI_End_User_trial
- Document_Information_Extraction_UI_Document_Viewer_trial
- Document_Information_Extraction_UI_Templates_Admin_trial
- Document_Information_Extraction_Attr_Dox_Client_Id_trial
 
Manual Actions Already Performed:
 
1. Created a custom role collection.
2. Added all available Document AI roles to the role collection.
3. Assigned the role collection to my user.
4. Logged out and logged back in.
 
Result:
 
When I open the tenant UI URL, I receive:
 
"401 Sorry, you are not authorized to use this application with tenant 9e0da020trial."
 
Additional Findings:
 
1. Under Services → Instances and Subscriptions → Subscriptions, I only see:
- SAP Build Code
- SAP HANA Cloud
- SAP Business Application Studio
- SAP Build Work Zone
 
SAP Document AI Trial Basic UI is NOT present.
 
2. Under Security → Trust Configuration → Establish Trust, I get:
"No tenants available"
 
3. In Service Marketplace, SAP Document AI Trial is available and service instances can be created, but there is no:
- Subscribe
- Create Subscription
- Go to Application
option available.
 
Questions:
 
1. Is the missing SAP Document AI Trial Basic UI subscription the reason for the 401 error?
2. Can the UI subscription be created manually after the booster has failed?
3. Is this a known limitation or issue in SAP BTP Trial accounts?
4. Does SAP Document AI Trial UI require IAS/OIDC trust even when the service instance, service key and roles already exist?
5. Has anyone successfully resolved this without modifying the subaccount identity provider configuration?
 
Any guidance or workaround would be greatly

entry.title: Re: API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 2: API Governance with Developer Hub
entry.content:
We are currently evaluating the transition from Classic API Management to the new API-Centric Integration capabilities running on the Integration Cell runtime.
It looks like classic API proxies cannot be deployed directly to the Integration Cell and must be manually recreated as the new API Artifact format.
Could the product team or community clarify:
  1. Is there an official migration tool or utility to convert classic proxies to the new API Artifact format?
  2. If manual recreation is the only option, are there any best practices or tools to speed up this process?
  3. What is the roadmap for automated migration support?

entry.title: Re: SO, DO , Billing Status on Document Flow
entry.content:
Can you confirm that you have already verified

  • that the “Update Document Flow” option is set to “X” in the copy control settings for VTAA (order/order), VTLA (order/delivery), and VTAF (invoice/credit memo)?
  • Also verify the accuracy of the quantity settings and other relevant parameters in the copy control related to billing (VTFF).
  • No relevant OSS notes available for your exact version

If the status is correct on each document, you could improve the display of the document flow.

  • 2449464 - How to enhance the SD Document Flow
